comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around how people trust large language models (LLMs) without critical evaluation, comparing this behavior to trusting search engines or unreliable sources. Commenters suggest acceptance, caution, and mental health awareness when dealing with such trust issues.

comments.author: paul_n
comments.text:
Accept this this is going to continue to happen, ask yourself if it’s something in your control or not, and try to find a way to enjoy the ride. It’s going to be bumpy, as we’re going through trust issues outside of just LLMs as a society right now.

However, if I notice a friend is about to harm themselves in some way I’ll pull open their ChatGPT and show them directly how sycophantic it is by going completely 180 on what they prompted. It’s enough to make them second guess.

Sometimes I’ll ask them to pause and ask their gut first, but people are already disconnected from their own truths.

It’s going to be bumpy. Save your mental health.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced and pragmatic approach to dealing with trust in LLMs, emphasizing mental health and realistic expectations.

comments.author: sodapopcan
comments.text: Are you talking about people who will still insist the LLM was correct even after being presented with evidence to the contrary, or people who don't EVER bother double checking answers they get out of said software since they assume it to be true?
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully distinguishes between different types of trust in LLMs, highlighting the importance of critical evaluation.

comments.author: kace91
comments.text:
Honestly, the kind of people doing that is probably better served by AI (currently).

I'm saying that because they were not going to be critical of the search results, and google is not exactly showing objective truth in the first positions nowadays.
comments.analysis: The comment argues that people who uncritically trust LLMs might benefit from AI more than traditional search engines, noting issues with search result objectivity.

comments.author: chipgap98
comments.text: Is this any different than people who believe random things they read on sketchy news sites or social media?
comments.analysis: This comment draws a valid analogy between trusting LLMs and trusting unreliable information sources, emphasizing a broader issue of information literacy.

comments.author: renewiltord
comments.text: There are two kinds of fools in the world: the kind who ask a search engine and believe the first reputable source they see, and the kind who ask an LLM and believe the first response that has a reputable citation.
comments.analysis: This comment uses a blunt tone to highlight similar trust issues with both search engines and LLMs, though it is somewhat dismissive.

comments.author: max8539
comments.text: asking ChatGPT to read and tell me what this post is about
comments.analysis: This comment adds no substantive value to the discussion and appears to be a lighthearted or trolling remark.
